name: Johnny Griffin
abstract: John Arnold Griffin III (April 24, 1928 – July 25, 2008) was an American jazz tenor saxophonist. Nicknamed "the Little Giant" for his short stature and forceful playing, Griffin began his career in the mid-1940s and continued until the month of his death.

concerts.label: Bel Canto Foundation Jazz Concert to Benefit Musicians
Kool Jazz Festival: The Blakey Legacy

about_quotes.speaker: Slide Hampton
about_quotes.evidence: That band, Joe Haider, we were co-leaders... Johnny Griffin... Dexter was in that band, and Benny Bailey was in that band, Johnny Griffin... I got Dexter, Johnny Griffin, Benny Bailey, Art Farmer. The whole band was like that... I wrote the music for the band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Slide Hampton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Percy Heath
about_quotes.evidence: Johnny Griffin was on tenor... We went on the road a couple of weeks with that band... Johnny Griffin, who I had met and had played with in Joe Morris's band
about_quotes.doc_title: Percy Heath — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: The 21st of June, I did a concert in Joinville, a suburb of Paris with Benny Carter, Ben Webster, Kenny Drew, Art Taylor, Michel Sarbadi, Johnny Griffin...

about_quotes.speaker: Michael Weiss
about_quotes.evidence: In all those years he never gave any specific instruction... getting back to Grif... you had to manage that and not get in the way and let things happen.
about_quotes.doc_title: Michael Weiss interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 22, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Melba Liston
about_quotes.evidence: He said, 'Can I bring some lead sheets up to your house and you make them for me?' ... And I wrote them. ... Johnny Griffin, and Ray Copeland.
about_quotes.doc_title: Melba Liston — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Arnett Cobb
about_quotes.evidence: And right on -- when Johnny Griffin come in, we fought on... we kept that same pattern for two years, traveling in the theater.
